comment_text:
Sounds like an education issue more than an issue with coca-cola itself, take away the coca-cola and then they'll all start drinking beer. In reality everyone in the documentary acts like the coca-cola itself causes diabetes when diabetes is a lot more complicated than that, like my parents have type 2 diabetes but they haven't touched a sugary soda in decades, while it's definitely not good for diabetes and can trigger a diabetic emergency, if it caused diabetes than alot of other things would too.

For one thing strangely rather than installing water treatment plants they resort to taxing sugar knowing that the public is going to continue buying it anyway, they just used it as an excuse to extort revenue.

comment_text: This is an identical story to Mauritius where my father was born. Coca cola inundated the country from the 1950's and after independence in the 1960s. They used the same model as in Mexico. The number of people dying from high blood pressure and type 2 diabetes rose dramatically. Multiple generations are now addicted to soft drinks and coca-cola phoenix beverages including phoenix beers every day. Sadly this is another form of sugar colonisation at the detriment and purposeful suppression of the local community.

comment_text:
Back in the 60's, I used to envy my cousin's house because they had cases of soft drinks from Mexico and all kinds of sugary snacks. My parents never bought those things for us. We only ate some sweets at weddings or parties. How thankful I am now. All my cousin's lives were cut short from diabetes, lost limbs. And here we are, grown kids, now ages 70 thru 84, but no diabetes!
I also want to add, the town's doctor doesn't look healthy either. But if you don't have clean water, can you blame the people? What other choice do they have?
